applicable_regulations: HIPAA (Privacy, Security, and Breach Notification Rules)
State health privacy laws (e.g., California CCPA/CPRA, New York SHIELD Act)
Potential FDA regulations only if the platform is classified as a medical device software (SaMD)
ip_defensibility: Moderate. The platform likely relies on standard software stacks and possibly proprietary workflows. IP protection hinges on obtaining clear copyrights on code, filing patents on any novel data‑handling algorithms, and rigorous open‑source license compliance. Without a strong patent portfolio or trade‑secret strategy, defensibility is limited.
critical_red_flags: No evidence of a formal HIPAA compliance program (risk assessments, policies, training, incident‑response plan).
Missing or incomplete Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) with any third‑party cloud or analytics providers.
Potential use of open‑source components without proper attribution or license compatibility (e.g., GPL code in a commercial product).
Unclear data‑storage locations – could trigger cross‑border data‑transfer issues under GDPR and state‑level restrictions.
Lack of documented encryption-at‑rest/in‑transit and access‑control mechanisms meeting HITRUST/SOC 2 standards.
No stated strategy for handling patient consent and data‑subject rights requests (opt‑out, deletion, data portability).
If the product is marketed as a clinical decision‑support tool, absence of FDA/21 CFR Part 820 QMS could expose the firm to enforcement actions.
mitigation_requirements: Develop and implement a HIPAA compliance framework: risk analysis, policies, staff training, breach‑notification procedures, and regular audits.
Execute BAAs with all cloud, analytics, and third‑party service providers handling PHI.
Conduct a comprehensive open‑source audit; replace or re‑license any incompatible components; maintain an SPDX‑style bill of materials.
Document data residency and ensure any cross‑border transfers are covered by Standard Contractual Clauses or Privacy Shield equivalents.
Adopt industry‑standard security controls: AES‑256 encryption at rest, TLS 1.2+ in transit, multi‑factor authentication, role‑based access, and logging for audit trails.
Implement a consent‑management module that captures, stores, and enforces patient preferences and GDPR/CCPA data‑subject rights.
If the product will be used for clinical decision‑support, perform a regulatory classification assessment and, if needed, register with the FDA and implement a Quality Management System.
Secure IP: file provisional patents on any novel data‑processing methods, maintain strict access controls to source code, and draft robust copyright notices.
